No appearances, not contested, and the tentative ruling is adopted as follows:

Defendant Rudolph and Sletten, Inc.'s Motion for Summary Judgment is DENIED. Opposition filed by Plaintiffs. Reply filed by Defendant.

1. There are triable issues of material fact in this action with respect to Defendant. (See Plaintiff's Response to Separate Statement at 6:9-20.) Decedent's co-worker, Alex Nofte, testified: that Fischbach & Moore provided a type of mask that went over the ear (Parker Decl., Ex. H [Depo. of Alex Nofte, Dated Oct. 15, 2023] at 417:2:5); and that he and Decedent always wore this type of mask when they were working at Mary's Help Hospital (id. at 417:11-14). The Court cannot reasonably infer from this testimony that Defendant warned Decedent about asbestos, that Decedent accepted the warning by always wearing a mask, and that Defendant did not expose Decedent to any asbestos. These are triable issues of material fact for a jury to decide.

Order signed.

Judge: Rochelle C. East. Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda. Bailiff: Not Present. Not Reported. (301/RCE)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
Off Calendar Dec-07-2023 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
Not contested, not argued and tentative ruling adopted: Defendant Consolidated Electrical Distributors, Inc.'s Motion for Summary Judgment is DENIED. Opposition filed by Plaintiff. Reply filed by Defendant. Defendant fails to sustain its initial burden of showing Plaintiff does not possess and cannot reasonably obtain needed evidence of causation for the following reasons. (Aguilar v. Atl. Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 855.)

First, Defendant does not show Decedent's deposition testimony is inadmissible in this case. (See Aguilar, supra, 25 Cal.4th at p. 851 [discussing role of trial burden of proof on summary judgment]; Berroteran v. Super. Ct. (2022) 12 Cal.5th 867, 895 [discussing trial burden of proof regarding admissibility of deposition testimony].)

Further, Plaintiff responded to the Court's request for production of documents by referring to and incorporating "all documents and things identified in Plaintiff's responses to Defendant-specific discovery, Special Interrogatories and Requests for Production of Documents . . . ." (Bugatto Decl., Ex. G [Plaintiff's Responses to General Order Standard Requests for Production] at 4:27-5:4.) Defendant's moving papers fail to show it propounded special discovery to determine the full scope of Plaintiff's evidence and whether it is factually devoid in its entirety. (Weber v. John Crane, Inc. (2006) 143 Cal.App.4th 1433, 1442.) The Court declines to consider Plaintiff's responses to Defendant's special interrogatories included in Defendant's reply evidence. Defendant's separate statement did not refer to special discovery. Plaintiff did not file opposing evidence. Plaintiff was thus not fully advised of the issues to be addressed and was not given adequate notice of which facts to rebut to prevail. (San Diego Watercrafts, Inc. v. Wells Fargo Bank, N.A. (2002) 102 Cal.App.4th 308, 316.) The Court need not rule on Plaintiff's objections to Defendant's reply evidence.

Finally, the Court did not consider Undisputed Material Fact No. 12 in Defendant's separate statement for failure to cite evidence establishing the undisputed fact. (Cal. Rules of Court, rule 3.1350, subd. (d)(3).) Order signed in open court. Judge: Samuel K. Feng, Clerk: A. Mok. Not reported. (503/SKF)

Hearing held, argued and tentative ruling adopted: Plaintiffs' Motion for Order Granting Preference in Setting Case for trial, and Extending Discovery Cutoff is GRANTED under Code of Civil Procedure section 36(a). The last day for hearing summary judgment/adjudication motions is July 21, 2022. Summary judgment/adjudication motions shall be brought on regular notice pursuant to the relevant provisions of the C.C.P., unless the parties stipulate otherwise. The Court will allow a maximum of four summary judgment/adjudication motions per day to be calendared in this action. Before a party files and serves such motion, it must contact the Court's Calendar Clerk to make a reservation. Time to respond to written discovery that has not yet been served is shortened to 20 days. For written discovery that has already been served, responses are due within 20 days of this hearing or by the date determined by the C.C.P., whichever is earlier. If there is any kind of issue/dispute that requires meet and confer, such meet and confer shall not be made via email or letter. It shall occur in person or via telephone. Electronic service is considered the equivalent of personal service. Fact discovery cut-off date is July 8, 2022. Expert discovery cut-off date is July 22, 2022. All bankruptcy documents shall be turned over to defendants no later than, May 6, 2022. If Plaintiffs submit documents to bankruptcy trusts after this date, they must notify defendants of this no later than five days after submission. The fact discovery cut-off does not apply to bankruptcy documents.
